name: Should I replace all my windows at once if only a few are bad?

mainEntity.name: If only a few windows are failing, is it smarter to replace all of them now or just the worst ones?
mainEntity.text: If only a few windows are failing, is it smarter to replace all of them now or just the worst ones?

acceptedAnswer.text: If only a few windows are failing, replacing just those units can be a sensible short-term decision. It keeps the upfront cost lower and lets homeowners prioritize the worst leaks, rot, or operational problems first. Replacing all windows at once, however, can improve consistency in appearance, simplify scheduling, and sometimes reduce total labor costs compared with multiple separate projects. In coastal homes, it can also be smart to replace all windows if several units are near the end of their life or if the same moisture exposure affects the whole house. The right choice depends on budget, the age and condition of the remaining windows, and whether you care about a matched look. A contractor can help compare the cost of phased work versus a full-house project and explain which approach makes the most sense for your home.
acceptedAnswer.description: Replacing only the worst windows can save money now, but replacing all windows at once may give better consistency, lower total mobilization costs, and a more uniform look. The best choice depends on budget, condition, and whether the remaining windows are likely to fail soon.
